text.p: The classical or traditional grammar was the grammar of the ancient Greeks (Latin). This grammar was ‚Äòcase based‚Äô, that is, the function of the word varies in a sentence as whether a subject, an object or an indirect object. This means that the function of a word is based on the appropriateness of the inflections. English for specific purposes emerged after the classical or traditional grammar or description model and as such, the description model did not have or rather does not have a strong hold on ESP (English for specific purposes). However there are contributions of this description model that ESP largely drew from.

The first contribution of this description model is the Register analysis, which is among the development of ESP. The notion that language varies according to context gave rise to the need of a particular language for a particular language situation. Register analysis therefore proves relevant in designing a syllabus for a particular language context. However, the content of a syllabus cannot be solely based on the register analysis of the particular field in that, there can be similarities between one field and another. Take for instance, the teaching of simple sentences & compound sentences can be included in the syllabus of a science subject like Chemistry, so also simple and compound sentences can also be included in the syllabus of Grammar for English students. What this means is that although Register analysis is relevant in designing a syllabus for various fields or subjects, it should not be the only basis for selecting and sequencing items in a syllabus.

Another contribution of the classical description model is in the area of structures and forms. That is, the structures and forms of a language was introduced by this model and it‚Äôs description by the model is still being alluded to. English for Specific purposes also draws from this as learners are being taught the structure and forms of the language in order to fully understand the language. Mere teaching the language or a language without teaching its structure and forms is like teaching only what the language can do without teaching what basically holds the language. That is, the structure and form serves as the basis which should not in any way be neglected.

The structural description or structuralism came after the classical or traditional description and it proved useful to a language like English which is a word-ordered language. Its contributions to English from which ESP draws from is the ‚Äòslot and filter‚Äô form, which can also be referred to as the substitution table. Here, words are arranged in a way that some are left to be arranged, that is, learners are asked to find substitutes to the words arranged. They are to include words that can be used in place of the original word and then arrange them accordingly. This will enable them to think and carry out the task.

Another contribution of the structural description is the structural syllabus. In the structural syllabus, items to be taught are arranged in the form of the simple and immediately used ones are taught before the complex ones. This means that those forms or items that will seem relevant or useful to the immediate needs of the learners are taught before those that are not needed immediately. Teaching the simple items before the complex ones will enable learners learn faster and easily as learners approach a field with items that they are more familiar with. This will also make them interested in the teaching process, that is, it will ignite their motivation to learn.

Structural description also introduced the surface level of language description which deals with the competence performance one has in a language. That is, it is the surface level that accounts for the performance of a learner in a language. Though, the surface level accounts for the performance but something enables the surface level to be able to account for the performance and this is the deeper level of meaning. It is of good importance when one has proficiency in a language but it is also of great importance if one or the learner knows the rules that enable him or her to be able to generate rules from the deeper level. Thus, the grammar of a language is not the surface level of a language but the rules that enables learners to generate the surface level from the deeper level. The structural description however did not lay much emphasis on the deeper level but only the surface level of meaning.

p.deleted: "English for Specific purposes (ESP=) is not a variety of English but rather an approach to language learning in which all decisions as to content and method are based on learners reasons for learning".

text.p: ‚ÄúEnglish for specific purposes (ESP) is not a variety of English but rather an approach to language learning in which all decisions as to content and method are based on learners reason for learning.‚Ä?
Hutchinson et al 1987

The above quotation simply states what ESP is about, that is, the essence of ESP is to meet the specific needs of the learners. ESP asserts needs and strategies motivation in which subject matter and relevant skills are taught. That is, the needs of the learners are found out and met, the motivation of the learner is required to do this and the particular skill(s) the learner needs are taught.

General English is more like the English everyone is taught, the basics of the language. The structure, the forms that make up the language. That is, the rudiments of the English language. General English is more of the academic purpose, that is, it is usually learnt in school, it is taught to students.

English for specific purposes (ESP) and General English share some similarities in that learners of ESP and General English must or should know the rudiments of the language. ESP assumes that its learners must have a knowledge of the language, they are only in an ESP class because they need a skill to be proficient and efficient in their place of work. ESP learners are professionals who as I mentioned earlier needs English to meet their specific needs. Another similarity between ESP and general English is that in either case, a particular skill is taught. In general English all the four skills of listening, speaking, reading and writing are taught but in ESP a particular skill is taught. It still boils down to the fact that a skill is being taught whether all or one.

English for specific purposes (ESP) and general English also differ in some areas. ESP learners are intermediate or adult learners who are learning English to improve their proficiency and efficiency in their place of assignment, while general English learners are pupils, students who are learning English in order to be competent in the language. This shows that while ESP is geared towards commercial purposes, organising seminars for learners, General English is geared towards academic purposes.

ESP centers on language in context. It is particular about the context where the learners will use the language. It is geared towards making the learners excel well in their area of speciality. General English therefore is aimed at making the learners good users of the language, using it to excel in their academic pursuit and good communicators in the language.

In English for specific purposes (ESP), a particular skill is taught to the learner, this is the skill that the learner needs to succeed in his/her place of jurisdiction. The skill can either be a speaking, listening, writing or reading skill. The reading skill however is seldom taught to ESP learners. In General English on the other hand, all the four skills of reading, speaking, listening and writing are taught to the learners, this is because they are expected to be an ‚Äòall rounder‚Äô, that is to know and excel in all the skills.

ESP is usually learner centered as against General English that is language centered. That is, it is the learner that is the focus of the ESP expert, the learner is what is at the back of his mind, how to teach the learner and enable him or her to excel and for his or her needs to be met. In the General English on the other hand, it is the language that is paramount to the teacher, how to teach the language to the students to make them comprehend it.

ESP learners approach the study field through the areas that they are familiar with. The interest of the learners is ignited once they see the need use of the learning learning to meet their immediate needs. General English is however not for immediate needs, it can be for future needs.
While general English is taught in school, ESP can be taught in halls, hotels, as long as the environment is conducive for learning.
a) The origin of ESP
Three factors are responsible for the origin of ESP and they are:
1. A brave new world
2. A revolution in linguistics
3. A focus on the learner

1. A brave new world: The end of the second world war in 1945 brought about an age of unprecedented expansion in the technical, economic and scientific activity which was unified by two dominating forces; technology and commerce. The unrelenting progress of these two forces saw the need of an international language. This role however fell on English. Prior to this time, people learnt a language without asking why they were learning it, they just learnt the language for the justification of learning it. People however saw the need for English as it was the language of the international currency. A new generation of people then came about as they saw the importance to learn English. Business men and women learnt in English so as to carry out their business successfully, doctors learnt English so as to write prescriptions accordingly, secretaries learnt English so as to write memos and letters well. The oil crisis of the 1970s also contributed to this development as their was a mass flow of funds into the Western and oil rich countries, English then became a big time business as people saw it as meeting their required goals.

2. A revolution in linguistics: Linguistics has traditionally been accustomed to describing the features of a language but the attention however shifted to communication uses, that is how language is used for communication. The guiding principle of ESP however became ‚Äòtell me what you need English for and I‚Äôll tell you the English you need.‚Ä?

3. A focus on the learner: The educational psychology at the until the 20th century shifted to the learner. The interests of the learner was taken into cognisance. The whole essence of the language was therefore based on the needs of the learner.
b) Target needs

Target needs are the needs a learner needs in order to function well in a target situation. The linguistic features of the target situation should be taken note of and aimed at in order for the learner to carry out his or her duties in the target situation. The target needs are divided into three, they are ;
1. Necessities
2. Lacks
3. Wants.
Necessities are what is needed in the target situation, that is what is in the target situation, that the learner doesn‚Äôt have.
Lacks are what the learner needs to succeed in the target situation, that is, what is missing in the learner.
Wants are what the learner requires in the target situation and they should not be overlooked.
